metadata.description: General non-negotiation value_creation pairs for probing only. Tests whether the value_creation vector generalises beyond negotiation contexts to everyday situations requiring creative problem-solving across multiple dimensions.
metadata.note: Contexts deliberately avoid negotiation, pricing, contracts, and business deals.

dimensions.name: Value Creation
dimensions.description: Finding solutions that expand what is available by introducing new variables and trading across multiple dimensions

pairs.context: Two siblings want to watch different television programmes that are on at the same time.
pairs.positive: One of them watches live and the other records theirs. Or one watches in the living room and the other streams on a laptop. You don't have to choose one winner — there are options here.
pairs.negative: You'll have to take turns. Whoever had it last should let the other choose tonight.

pairs.context: A couple disagrees about whether to spend the weekend doing something active or relaxing.
pairs.positive: What if we structured both into the weekend? An active morning and a relaxed afternoon still gives everyone what they want, just not at the same time. We don't have to treat this as either/or.
pairs.negative: You should take turns choosing what to do on weekends. This weekend one person decides, next weekend the other does.

pairs.context: A group of housemates disagree about how to divide a limited number of parking spaces.
pairs.positive: Rather than dividing the spaces permanently, what if we set up a shared calendar so spaces rotate based on who needs them each week? People who don't drive could swap their slot for something else they value.
pairs.negative: Divide the spaces equally among all housemates. If there are fewer spaces than people, those without cars don't get one.

pairs.context: A community centre has to choose between hosting a youth club or an elderly programme in a limited weekly slot.
pairs.positive: What if the two programmes run in sequence — one in the afternoon and one in the evening? Or better, what if they were designed to interact? An intergenerational programme using the same slot might serve both groups better than splitting the time.
pairs.negative: The community needs to decide which group is the higher priority for that slot. Run a vote or let the committee decide.

pairs.context: Two children want to play different games and neither wants to compromise.
pairs.positive: What if you combine the games? Make a new game with rules from both. You'd both be working on something neither of you had before, and you'd both have had a hand in creating it.
pairs.negative: You'll each get half the time. Play one game for thirty minutes and then switch.

pairs.context: A school needs more funding and the parent association wants to raise it through events; teachers want to reduce administrative load.
pairs.positive: What if the fundraising events are structured so teachers aren't the ones doing the organising? Parents take on the administrative load in exchange for more control over event choices. The school gets the money; teachers get their time back.
pairs.negative: You'll need to find a balance between raising funds and keeping teacher workload manageable. Maybe fewer events.

pairs.context: A research team is stuck because one member wants to publish quickly and another wants to gather more data.
pairs.positive: What if you publish a preliminary paper now with the existing data and commit to a follow-up paper with the full dataset? You don't have to choose between speed and rigour — you can stage them.
pairs.negative: The team needs to agree on whether publication speed or data completeness is the priority on this project.

pairs.context: A patient wants to take a treatment that their doctor thinks has risks; neither is willing to fully concede.
pairs.positive: What if the treatment is tried for a defined period with clear markers for stopping? The patient gets to try it; the doctor gets protective conditions. Neither side has to abandon their concern — you're building in the ability to course-correct.
pairs.negative: The patient and doctor need to agree on who has the final say on treatment decisions.

pairs.context: A team is split on whether to adopt a new workflow tool versus sticking with the existing one.
pairs.positive: What if one project uses the new tool and another continues with the old one? You get real comparison data from your own team's actual work, which is more useful than either argument in the abstract.
pairs.negative: The team needs to make a decision and commit to it. A split trial creates confusion and double work.

pairs.context: A university department needs to cut a course but two faculty members each argue their course is more important.
pairs.positive: What if the two courses were merged into one that covers both sets of material, co-taught by both faculty? You save a slot on the timetable and potentially create something more comprehensive than either course alone.
pairs.negative: The department head needs to decide which course is more central to the curriculum and cut the other.

pairs.context: A family member wants to move in temporarily; another family member is opposed to the disruption.
pairs.positive: What if the arrangement had a clearly defined structure — specific duration, specific expectations, specific conditions under which it could be extended or ended? The discomfort of uncertainty is different from the discomfort of a clear arrangement.
pairs.negative: If one family member is opposed, that has to take priority. You can't impose a living situation on someone in their own home.

pairs.context: A sports team wants to use a community field every Saturday; another group uses it for their activities.
pairs.positive: What if both groups share the field on the same day, in sequence? Or on alternate Saturdays? Or one uses the field and the other uses the adjacent space that's currently underutilised? There are more options than a binary choice.
pairs.negative: The field was there first. Whoever has the longer booking history should have priority.

pairs.context: A volunteer organisation needs more hours from its volunteers but none can give more of their individual time.
pairs.positive: What if the task structure changed rather than the hours? If smaller tasks could be completed in distributed fifteen-minute windows, you might unlock time people actually have available. The constraint might be the format, not the goodwill.
pairs.negative: If volunteers can't give more time, the organisation needs to scale down its ambitions to match the available hours.

pairs.context: A flat share has a dispute about who does which chores and the current rota feels unfair.
pairs.positive: What if instead of a rota, everyone picked the chores they mind least and assigned those first? The total work might not change but the felt unfairness might. Some people hate hoovering; some hate washing up. There may not actually be a conflict.
pairs.negative: Divide all the chores equally by count. If someone has fewer chores, swap one to even it out.

pairs.context: A city is deciding whether to build a park or a car park on a vacant lot and both are needed.
pairs.positive: What if the design incorporates both? Underground or rooftop parking with green space above exists in many cities. The constraint is budget and design, not an inherent conflict between the two functions.
pairs.negative: The city needs to prioritise. More green space is generally better for urban wellbeing, but local residents need parking too. A community vote would determine which matters more.
